title: Telugu Alphabets (అక్షరమాల)
content: Telugu is written in its own script. Learn the basic vowels (అచ్చులు) and consonants (హల్లులు) with examples.
intro: The Telugu script has 16 vowels (including long forms and diphthongs) and around 36 basic consonants. Each consonant carries an inherent vowel అ (a) unless changed by a vowel sign (maatralu). Below are the main vowels and consonants with transliteration and usage examples.

title: Vowels and Consonants in Telugu and their pronunciation
content: Understand how Telugu vowels and consonants sound and how they are grouped.
intro: Telugu letters are arranged from the back of the mouth to the lips. Vowels have short and long forms (అ/ఆ, ఇ/ఈ, ఉ/ఊ, ఎ/ఏ, ఒ/ఓ) and consonants come in voiced/unvoiced, aspirated/unaspirated pairs. Practising minimal pairs helps you hear the differences.

title: Telugu Barakhadi: Vowel signs with consonants (గుణింతాలు)
content: See how vowel signs (maatralu) attach to consonants in Telugu.
intro: In Telugu, consonants combine with vowel signs (గుణింతాలు) to form full syllables. Below we show consonant క (ka) and some other consonants with all vowel signs.

tables.heading: Vowel signs with గ (ga) – few examples

title: Frequently faced pronunciation problems in Telugu
content: Common pronunciation difficulties for non-native speakers of Telugu.
intro: Learners often confuse short/long vowels (అ/ఆ, ఇ/ఈ), dental vs retroflex sounds (త/ట, ద/డ) and the different "sha" and "la" sounds. The table below shows typical pairs.

title: Pronunciation of Anusvara (ం), Chandrabindu (ఁ) and Visarga (ః)
content: Learn how to read and pronounce nasal and breathy markers in Telugu.
intro: Telugu uses ం (anusvāra), ఁ (chandrabindu – rarely) and ః (visarga) to mark nasalisation or a soft "h" sound. Their pronunciation depends on the following consonant.

tables.heading: Chandrabindu (ఁ) – nasalised vowel (rare in modern Telugu)

title: Combining consonants in Telugu (సమ్యుక్తాక్షరాలు)
content: Learn how consonant clusters (conjuncts) are written in Telugu.
intro: Two or more consonants can combine to form సమ్యుక్తాక్షరాలు (conjunct consonants). The second consonant is shown with a special half‑form without the inherent vowel.

title: Combining consonants with ర (ra) – Part 2
content: Special conjuncts involving ర (ra) in Telugu.
intro: When ర combines with other consonants, it can appear before or after them in a compact ligature: క్ర, గ్ర, ప్ర, బ్ర, ట్ర, ద్ర etc. Get used to recognising these shapes.

title: Combining consonants with హ (ha) – Part 3
content: Conjuncts where హ joins other consonants.
intro: హ combines with other letters like క, గ, భ to give a breathy or aspirated feel in cluster: ఖ, ఘ, భ, ధ etc., and also appears in conjuncts like జ్హ, శ్హ in some Sanskritic words.

title: Special or frequently used combined consonant patterns
content: Some special or common conjuncts and ligatures you will see often in Telugu.
intro: A few clusters like క్ష, జ్ఞ, శ్రీ and స్త్ర appear very frequently in Telugu, especially in Sanskrit‑derived words and names. Recognising them makes reading much easier.

title: Pronouns and Articles in Telugu
content: Learn personal pronouns and basic articles in Telugu.
intro: Telugu does not have separate articles like "a/an/the" in English, but uses demonstratives and context. Personal pronouns change by person (I, you, he/she, we, they) and respect.

title: Using plurals to indicate respect in Telugu
content: Learn how plural forms are used to show respect in Telugu.
intro: Like many Indian languages, Telugu uses the plural pronoun మీరు (mīru) and plural verb forms to show respect to elders or strangers.

title: Verbs in Telugu
content: Overview of verb roots and infinitive forms in Telugu.
intro: Telugu verb roots usually end in a consonant; the basic dictionary form is often shown with -డు (for masculine) or neutral stem. Infinitives commonly end in -డం / -డం, -టం etc.

title: Simple Present Tense in Telugu
content: Form and use of the simple present tense in Telugu.
intro: Simple present in Telugu often uses the stem + తాడు/తుంది/తాయి/తారు etc. It is used for habitual actions and general truths.

title: Simple Present Tense of "To Be" in Telugu
content: Learn how existence and identity are expressed in Telugu.
intro: In colloquial Telugu, the copula "is" is often dropped in the present tense. The verb ఉండటం (to be/stay) is used for existence, possession and location.

tables.heading: To be – present tense examples
rows: The book is on the table.
There is water in the bottle.

title: Present Continuous Tense in Telugu
content: Form and use of the present continuous tense in Telugu.
intro: Present continuous describes an action going on now. In colloquial Telugu it is often formed with verb stem + తున్నాను/తున్నావు/తున్నాడు…

tables.heading: Example sentences – present continuous
rows: We are waiting for the bus.
I am speaking with my friend.

title: Simple Future Tense in Telugu
content: Form and use of the simple future tense in Telugu.
intro: Simple future is usually made with verb stem + తాను/తావు/తాడు/తుంది/తారు. It is used for future plans and predictions.

tables.heading: Future tense – example sentences
rows: She will go to the market.
They will buy a new car.

title: Future Continuous Tense in Telugu
content: Form and use of the future continuous tense in Telugu.
intro: Future continuous can be expressed with verb + తూ ఉంటాను/ఉంటారు etc., literally "will be doing".

tables.heading: Future continuous – examples
rows: We will be waiting for you.
I will be reading at that time.
I will be writing a report.
We will be walking in the park.

title: Simple Past Tense in Telugu – Part 1 (Verbs without object)
content: Simple past tense forms for intransitive verbs in Telugu.
intro: For many intransitive verbs, the simple past is formed from the verb root with endings like -అను/-ఆను etc. Patterns vary by verb.

title: Simple Past Tense in Telugu – Part 2 (Verbs with object)
content: Simple past tense for transitive verbs in Telugu.
intro: For many transitive verbs, past forms end in -య్/-ఇన్ etc. Usage depends on subject and object.

title: Simple Past Tense in Telugu – Part 3 (Past tense of "to be")
content: Past forms of "to be" (ఉండటం) in Telugu.
intro: The verb ఉండటం has past forms like ఉండేవాడు, ఉంది, ఉండేవారు etc., used for "was/were".

title: Exceptional Verbs in Telugu which change in past tense form
content: Some Telugu verbs have irregular past forms.
intro: A few common verbs do not follow the simple pattern in past tense. It is best to memorise them.

title: Past Continuous Tense in Telugu
content: Action that was ongoing in the past.
intro: Past continuous can be expressed with verb + తూ ఉన్నాను/ఉన్నాడు etc., literally "was doing".

title: Present/Past/Future Perfect Tense in Telugu
content: Perfect tenses use completed action forms in Telugu.
intro: Perfect tenses often use the past participle + ఉండి/ఉంటూ/ఉంటాడు etc. There are several colloquial patterns.

title: Learn Prepositions in Telugu
intro: Like other Indian languages, Telugu mostly uses postpositions that come after the noun: లో (in), పై (on), వద్ద (at), కోసం (for), నుంచి (from) etc.

tables.heading: Common postpositions
rows: The book is in the bag.
The pen is on the table.
I am at the bus stop.

title: Preposition "TO" in Telugu
content: Different ways of expressing "to" in Telugu.
intro: "To" in English can mean direction (to the market), indirect object (to him), or purpose (to eat). Telugu uses different postpositions or case endings for these meanings.

tables.heading: "To" as direction and recipient
rows: She is going to the market.
I gave money to my friend.
They are going to the cinema.
We are going to the park.
He is walking to the office.
I am going to my house.

title: Sentences with person or living things as object in Telugu
content: How to mark objects that are people or living things.
intro: Often the dative marker కి/కు is used with persons, while things can take no marker or other postpositions.

tables.heading: Object is a person – examples
rows: They are waiting for the doctor.

title: Saying My/His/Her in Telugu
content: Possessive forms for my, your, his, her etc. in Telugu.
intro: Possession is often shown with నా (my), నీ (your), అతని (his), ఆమె (her) etc., before the noun.

title: Basic questions and "WH" questions in Telugu
content: Learn WH-words like who, what, where, when in Telugu.
intro: Telugu has separate words for who (ఎవరు), what (ఏమి/ఏంటి), where (ఎక్కడ), when (ఎప్పుడు), why (ఎందుకు), how (ఎలా).

title: Negative Sentences – Present tense in Telugu
content: Forming negative sentences in the present tense.
intro: Colloquial Telugu often uses లేదు (lēdu) after the verb phrase for present negatives.

title: Negative Sentences – Past tense in Telugu
intro: Past negatives often use లేద (lēdu) or లేను (lēnu) with the past participle.

title: Negative Sentence – Future Tense in Telugu
intro: Future negatives can be formed with verb + ను/డు + కాదు or with వద్దు for "should not/will not" nuance.

title: Working with nouns – Gender & Plurals in Telugu
content: Basics of gender and plural formation with Telugu nouns.
intro: Many Telugu nouns have masculine/feminine pairs; plurals often end in -లు (-lu).

title: Working with nouns – Prepositions in Telugu
intro: Combine nouns with లో, పై, వద్ద, నుంచి, కోసం etc. to express location, direction and purpose.

title: Prepositions with similar pronunciation or similar meanings in Telugu
content: Prepositions/postpositions that sound or mean similar things.
intro: Words like లో/లోపల (in/inside), పై/మీద (on/over), వద్ద/దగ్గర (near/at) can confuse learners.

tables.heading: Similar postpositions – comparison
rows: పుస్తకం బ్యాగ్ లో ఉంది. / పాము గుంత లోపల ఉంది.
pustakaṃ byāg lō undi. / pāmu gunta lōpala undi.
పుస్తకం మేజాపై ఉంది. / దుమ్ము టేబుల్ మీద ఉంది.
pustakaṃ mējāpai undi. / dum̄mu ṭēbul mīda undi.
అతని వద్ద డబ్బు లేదు. / స్కూల్ దగ్గర దుకాణం ఉంది.
atani vadda ḍabbu lēdu. / skūl daggara dukāṇaṃ undi.

title: Cases in Telugu (విభక్తులు)
content: Overview of basic case endings (vibhaktulu) in Telugu.
intro: Telugu uses case endings for subject, object, instrument, location etc. like -ము/-ము (nominative), -ను (accusative), -తో (instrumental), -లో (locative), -కి/కు (dative).

title: Commands / Imperative statements in Telugu
content: How to give commands and requests in Telugu.
intro: Imperatives can be casual (వెళ్లు, రా), polite (వెళ్ళండి, రండి) or very polite (వెళ్ళండి సర్).

title: Time related words in Telugu
content: Common words about time in Telugu.
intro: Learn today, yesterday, tomorrow, morning, evening, always, sometimes, never etc.

tables.heading: Time vocabulary and examples
rows: She will come in the evening.

title: Verbs indicating cause of action. Causative verb in Telugu
content: Causative forms express "make someone do" something.
intro: Causatives can be formed with -ించు (-in̄cu) and related patterns: చదవించు (make read), తినిపించు (feed).

tables.heading: Causative verb examples
rows: She made the child drink milk.

title: Vowel‑pattern style rule in Telugu (sound patterns)
content: Some verbs and nouns change sound with regular patterns in Telugu.
intro: Like A‑I‑e‑e‑yA‑I type rules in Marathi, Telugu has patterns where vowels change in related forms.

title: Past/Present/Future Perfect Tense – Second Style in Telugu
content: Alternative perfect patterns used in spoken Telugu.
intro: Colloquial Telugu uses forms like చేసి ఉన్నాను, చేసి ఉండేవాడిని etc. for different time frames.

title: Past/Present/Future Perfect Continuous tense in Telugu
content: Perfect continuous forms combine completion and duration.
intro: These can be expressed with combinations like చేసుకుంటూ వచ్చాను (have been doing and came) in colloquial Telugu.

tables.heading: Perfect continuous – examples
rows: I have been working here for two years.
నేను ఇక్కడ రెండు సంవత్సరాలుగా పని చేస్తున్నాను.
nēnu ikkaḍa reṇḍu samvatsarāluga pani cēstunnānu.
He had been waiting for a long time.
అతను చాలా సేపటి గా ఎదురుచూస్తూ ఉన్నాడు.
atanu chālā sēpaṭi gā edurucūstū unnāḍu.
They will have been living in Hyderabad for ten years.
వారు పది సంవత్సరాలుగా హైదరాబాద్ లో ఉంటూ ఉంటారు.
vāru padi samvatsarāluga haidarābād lō uṇṭū uṇṭāru.
She has been studying for three hours.
We had been talking for an hour.
మేము ఒక గంట పాటు మాట్లాడుకుంటూ ఉన్నాం.
mēmu oka gaṇṭa pāṭu māṭlāḍukuntū unnāṃ.
I have been learning Telugu for a year.
He had been sleeping since morning.
They have been playing since morning.
She will have been teaching for five years.
We have been waiting for two hours.
I had been reading all day.
He has been cooking since morning.
They had been living there for years.
వారు అక్కడ చాలా సంవత్సరాలుగా ఉంటూ ఉన్నారు.
vāru akkaḍa chālā samvatsarālugā uṇṭū unnāru.
She has been singing for an hour.
We will have been working for ten years.
మేము పది సంవత్సరాలుగా పని చేస్తూ ఉంటాము.
mēmu padi samvatsarālugā pani cēstū uṇṭāmu.

title: Difference between కాదు (kādu) / వద్దు (vaddhu) and "don’t want"
content: Learn when to use కాదు and when to use వద్దు.
intro: కాదు (kādu) usually negates a noun or statement (is not), while వద్దు (vaddhu) expresses refusal / "don’t want" / "don’t do".

title: Sentence using "Going to" phrase in Telugu
intro: Practice patterns.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I am going to eat now.
We are going to watch a movie.
He is going to call you.
They are going to Hyderabad tomorrow.
I am not going to buy this.
She is going to start a new job.
We are going to meet at the park.
He is going to write a letter.
I am going to learn Telugu.
She is going to cook dinner.
They are going to travel by train.
Are we going to wait here?
I am going to finish this work.
He is going to read the book.

title: Sentence using "Used to" phrase in Telugu
intro: ముందు + verb.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I used to live in Hyderabad.
She used to work in a bank.
ఆమె ముందు బ్యాంక్ లో పని చేసేదీ.
āme mundu byānk lō pani cēsēdī.
We used to play here every day.
mēmu mundu prati rōju ikkaḍa āḍutuṇḍēvāḷḷaṃ.
They used to travel by bus.
వారు ముందు బస్ లో ప్రయాణం చేసేవారు.
vāru mundu bas lō prayāṇaṃ cēsēvāru.
Did you use to get up early?
I did not use to eat spicy food.
నేను ముందు ఎక్కువ మసాలా తినేవాడిని కాదు.
nēnu mundu ekkuva masālā tinēvāḍini kādu.
She used to read a lot.
He used to walk to school.
We used to meet on Sundays.
They used to speak Telugu at home.
I used to watch TV every evening.
నేను ముందు ప్రతి సాయంత్రం టీవీ చూసేవాడిని.
nēnu mundu prati sāyantraṃ ṭīvī cūsēvāḍini.
He used to help his mother.
అతను ముందు తన అమ్మకు సహాయం చేసేవాడు.
atanu mundu tana ammaku sahāyaṃ cēsēvāḍu.
We used to go to the temple.

title: Sentence using "Used to" phrase – old style in Telugu
intro: Practice.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I used to go there often.
She used to cook for us.
He used to wake up at five.
We used to have breakfast together.
They used to visit us every month.
He used to run in the morning.
We used to wait for the bus here.
I used to love this place.
He used to drive a car.
They used to live in a village.

title: Sentence using "If-Then" in Telugu
intro: ఒకవేళ / ఐతే.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: If it rains, we will not go.
If you come early, we will talk.
If I get time, I will call you.
నాకు టైం ఉంటే, నేను నీకు ఫోన్ చేస్తాను.
nāku ṭaim uṇṭē, nēnu nīku phōn cēstānu.
If he studies well, he will pass.
If you are tired, take rest.
నీవు అలసిపోయి ఉంటే, కొంచెం విశ్రాంతి తీసుకో.
nīvu alasipōyi uṇṭē, konchem viśrānti tīsukō.
If they invite us, we will go.
If there is traffic, we will be late.
If you don't understand, ask again.
If she agrees, we will start.
If the price is low, I will buy.
If you need help, tell me.
If he comes, we will be happy.
If it is true, I am sorry.
If the bus comes, we will board.
If you work hard, you will succeed.

title: Prepositions with Verbs in Telugu
intro: Practice.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I am waiting for the bus.
She is talking about the movie.
I am looking for my keys.
We are proud of our country.

title: Adjectives in Telugu
intro: Practice.

title: Can / To be able to in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I wasn't able to phone yesterday.
Will she be able to cope?

title: To Want / To Need in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: ఆమెకి ఎదురుచూస్తూ ఉండడం అంటే ఇష్టం లేదు.
āmeki edurucūstū uṇḍaḍaṃ aṇṭē iṣṭaṃ lēdu.
He wants to speak to you.

title: To Become / To Happen in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

title: Should in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

title: Must / Have to in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: He has to pay the bill.
I have to call my parents.
నేను నా తల్లిదండ్రులకు ఫోన్ చేయాల్సి ఉంది.
nēnu nā talli daṇḍrilaku phōn cēyālsi undi.
He has to submit the form.

title: to keep doing in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

title: Comparison in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: ఈ రోజు నిన్న కంటే ఎక్కువ వేడిగా ఉంది.
ī rōju ninna kaṇṭē ekkuva vēḍigā undi.
Hyderabad is bigger than our town.

title: To Know in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: He came to know the truth.
Do you know how to drive?

title: Let and Shall we in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: Shall we go for a walk?

title: Which-That in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: This is the book that I bought.
The man who came is my friend.

title: Giving instructions in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: దయచేసి మీ మొబైల్ స్విచ్ ఆఫ్ చేయండి.
dayacēsi mī mobail svic āph cēyaṇḍi.
Enter your name in capital letters.
మీ పేరు పెద్ద అక్షరాల్లో నమోదు చేయండి.
mī pēru peḍḍa akṣarāllō namōdu cēyaṇḍi.

title: To Like in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: ఆమెకి కారంగా ఉన్న ఆహారం ఇష్టం లేదు.
āmeki kāraṅgā unna āhāraṃ iṣṭaṃ lēdu.

title: Would in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I would help if I had time.
Would you mind closing the door?
I knew you would say this.
We would go if it rained.

title: To Understand in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I came to know the truth.
We need to understand each other.
I came to know from the news.

title: Question tag in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: You are coming tomorrow, aren't you?
This is your bag, isn't it?
It is hot today, isn't it?
We will go by bus, okay?
She is a teacher, isn't she?
He can speak Telugu, can't he?

title: To remember in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

title: To Want with other verbs in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: We need to finish the work.
They want to watch a movie.
Do you want to drink tea?
She wants to buy a dress.
He needs to call his parents.
She wants to read this book.
He wants to speak to you.

title: To Want with other person in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: We want them to stay here.
My parents want me to study.
I want you to come here.
They want us to visit them.
Do you want me to call you?
I want everyone to be happy.
We want the children to eat.
Parents want children to tell the truth.
I don't want her to go alone.
Boss wants us to finish today.

title: supposed to in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: I am supposed to go now.
He was supposed to call me.
We were supposed to meet yesterday.
You are supposed to submit the form.
The bus is supposed to come at six.
She is supposed to be here.
They were supposed to finish this work.
Guests are supposed to arrive tomorrow.
You were not supposed to tell this.
He is supposed to pay the bill.
Students are supposed to wear uniform.
The meeting was supposed to start at ten.
I was supposed to leave early.
She is supposed to cook today.

tables.heading: Examples

ఆమె నన్ను ఆమె కుటుంబానికి పరిచయం చేసింది.
āme nannu āme kuṭumbānikī paricayaṃ cēsindi.
They offered water to the guests.

title: Conjunctions 1 in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: This is beautiful but that is not.
ఇది అందంగా ఉంది కానీ అది కాదు.
idi andaṅgā undi kānī adi kādu.
I am happy because I saw you.
It was raining so we stayed.
I know that he will come.
We left early because of traffic.
ట్రాఫిక్ ఉన్నందుకు మేము త్వరగా బయటకు వెళ్లాము.
ṭrāphik unnaṃduku mēmu tvaragā bayataku veḷḷāmu.
He was tired so he slept.

title: Conjunctions 2 in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: If you come we will go.
If it rains we will stay.
As long as you are here.
If you want I will come.

title: Conjunctions 3 in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: As long as you are here.
As far as I know he is good.
After the meeting we will go.
As long as there is hope.
As far as the eye can see.
As long as you are happy.

title: Conjunctions 4 in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: As soon as I heard I came.
Once you start you will like it.
Rather than walking take a bus.
As soon as the bell rang.
Rather than tea I want coffee.
Once you learn you won't forget.

title: Combining sentences in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: He left because he was tired.
She studied hard so she passed.
We ate and then we left.
It's raining so take an umbrella.
I called because I needed help.
They were hungry so they ate.
వారికి ఆకలి ఉంది కాబట్టి వారు తిన్నారు.
vāriki ākali undi kābaṭṭi vāru tinnāru.
I know that he is honest.
We waited but he didn't come.
నువ్వు సహాయం చేసినందుకు నేను విజయం సాధించాను.
nuvvu sahāyaṃ cēsinaṃduku nēnu vijayaṃ sādhin̄cānu.
She is tired so she is resting.
He ran and caught the bus.

title: If-Then Style 2 in Telugu
intro: Practice topic-specific Telugu sentences with English and transliteration.

tables.heading: Examples
rows: Had I known I would have come.
Were he here we would go.
Had she invited I would have gone.
If I had money I would buy.
Had she studied she would have passed.
Had she known she would have told.
If you had asked I would have helped.
నువ్వు అడిగి ఉంటే నేను సహాయం చేసేవాడిని.
nuvvu aḍigi uṇṭē nēnu sahāyaṃ cēsēvāḍini.
Had she waited we would have met.
If he had tried he would have succeeded.
Were you here I would tell.
Had they known they would have come.
If I had time I would visit.

title: Diwali Festival in Telugu
content: Diwali (దీపావళి) is the festival of lights celebrated across India.
intro: Diwali is the festival of lights. Learn Telugu phrases and wishes for Diwali.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: Diwali is the festival of lights.
Happy Diwali to you and your family.
మీకు మీ కుటుంబ సభ్యులకు దీపావళి శుభాకాంక్షలు.
mīku mī kuṭumba sabhyulaku dīpāvaḷi śubhākāṃkṣalu.
I bought new clothes for Diwali.
నేను దీపావళి కోసం కొత్త బట్టలు కొన్నాను.
nēnu dīpāvaḷi kōsaṃ kotta baṭṭalu konnānu.
Diwali symbolizes victory of good over evil.
దీపావళి మంచిపై చెడు సాధించిన విజయానికి ప్రతీక.
dīpāvaḷi maṃcipai ceḍu sādhin̄cina vijayāniki pratīka.
దీపావళి ముందు మీ ఇంటిని శుభ్రం చేయండి.
dīpāvaḷi muṇdu mī iṇṭini śubhraṃ cēyaṇḍi.
Diwali falls in October or November.
దీపావళి అక్టోబర్ లేదా నవంబర్ లో వస్తుంది.
dīpāvaḷi akṭōbar lēdā navaṃbar lō vastundi.

title: Sankranti Festival in Telugu
content: Sankranti (Makara Sankranti) is a major harvest festival in Telugu states.
intro: Sankranti (Makara Sankranti) is a major harvest festival in Telugu states.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: Bhogi brings prosperity, Sankranti brings joy.
భోగి మీకు భోగభాగ్యాలను, సంక్రాంతి మీకు సుఖసంతోషాలను.
bhōgi mīku bhōgabhāgyālanu, saṃkrāṃti mīku sukhasaṃtōṣālanu.
May your home fill with happiness this Sankranti.
సంక్రాంతి సందర్భంగా మీ ఇల్లు సుఖసంతోషాలతో నిండిపోవాలని కోరుకుంటూ.
saṃkrāṃti saṃdarbhaṃgā mī illu sukhasaṃtōṣālatō niṇḍipōvālanī kōrukuṇṭū.
Makar Sankranti marks the sun's northward journey.
మకర సంక్రాంతి సూర్యుడి ఉత్తరాయణ ప్రయాణాన్ని సూచిస్తుంది.
makara saṃkrāṃti sūryuḍi uttarāyaṇa prayāṇānni sūcistundi.
May this Sankranti bring health and prosperity.
ఈ సంక్రాంతి మీకు ఆయురారోగ్యాలు, ఆనందం తీసుకురావాలని.
ī saṃkrāṃti mīku āyurārōgyālu, ānandaṃ tīsukuravālanī.
మేము సంక్రాంతి కోసం హరిదాసు తయారు చేస్తాము.
mēmu saṃkrāṃti kōsaṃ haridāsu tayāru cēstāmu.
Children wear new clothes on Sankranti.
పిల్లలు సంక్రాంతి నాడు కొత్త బట్టలు ధరిస్తారు.
pillalu saṃkrāṃti nāḍu kotta baṭṭalu dharistāru.
May Lakshmi bless you this Sankranti.
We decorate the house with rangoli.

prajā ravāṇā dvārā manaṃ akkaḍikī cērukōgalamā?
I want to get off here.
Where can I buy a ticket?
The shop is near the bank.
The temple is behind the market.
Can you show me the way?
The post office is opposite the bank.
How far is it from here?

intro: Conversation with auto or taxi driver.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: Where do you want to go?
I want to go to the station.
Please take me to this address.

dayacēsi nannu ī cirunāmā kī tīsukeḷḷaṇḍi.

title: Simple Telugu Conversation – Software Engineers
intro: Conversation between software engineers.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: Which project are you working on?

mīru ē prājekṭ pai pani cēstunnāru?
I am working on a mobile app.
నేను మొబైల్ యాప్ పై పని చేస్తున్నాను.
nēnu mobail yāp pai pani cēstunnānu.
Let's have tea in the break.

rows: May I go to the bathroom?

idi mī nōṭ buk lō rāyaṇḍi.

title: Simple Telugu conversation – Informal phone conversation
intro: Informal phone conversation.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: Shall we go for a movie?
I will call you before I leave.

nēnu veḷḷē muṇdu mīku kāl cēstānu.

intro: Conversation at vegetable market.

tables.heading: Conversation

nēnu ṭīm lō bāgā pani cēstānu.
I have three years of experience.
I look forward to hearing from you.

intro: Conversation with housemaid.

rows: The water is in the kitchen.

idi nēnu iṃtaku muṇdu ruci cūḍalēdu.

tadupari sāri ā cōṭu ṭrai cēddāṃ.

title: Simple Telugu conversation – Linking AADHAR card to mobile number
intro: Learn phrases for linking Aadhaar to mobile.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: I want to link my Aadhaar to my mobile.
నా ఆధార్ కార్డును మా మొబైల్ నంబర్ కు లింక్ చేయాలనుకుంటున్నాను.
nā ādhār kārḍunu mā mobail naṃbar ku lin̄k cēyālanukuṇṭunnānu.
How do I link Aadhaar to mobile?
ఆధార్ కార్డుకు మొబైల్ నంబర్ ఎలా లింక్ చేయవచ్చు?
ādhār kārḍuku mobail naṃbar elā lin̄k cēyavaccu?
Carry your Aadhaar card and mobile.
మీ ఆధార్ కార్డు మరియు మొబైల్ తీసుకురండి.
mī ādhār kārḍu mariyu mobail tīsukuraṇḍi.
OTP will be sent to your mobile.
I did not receive the OTP.
Go to the telecom operator store.

title: Simple Telugu conversation – Linking mobile number to AADHAR card
intro: Learn phrases for linking mobile to Aadhaar.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: I want to link my mobile to Aadhaar.
నా మొబైల్ నంబర్ ను ఆధార్ కు లింక్ చేయాలనుకుంటున్నాను.
nā mobail naṃbar nu ādhār ku lin̄k cēyālanukuṇṭunnānu.
Which number do you want to link?
Only one mobile can be linked.
ఆధార్ సెంటర్ లో మొబైల్ నంబర్ అప్‌డేట్ చేయండి.
ādhār seṇṭar lō mobail naṃbar apḍēṭ cēyaṇḍi.
The OTP is valid for 10 minutes.
I have linked my new number.
నేను నా కొత్త నంబర్ లింక్ చేశాను.
nēnu nā kotta naṃbar lin̄k cēśānu.
ఆన్‌లైన్ లో మీ ఆధార్ స్టేటస్ చెక్ చేయండి.
ānlain lō mī ādhār sṭēṭas cek cēyaṇḍi.

title: Simple Telugu conversation – Republic Day 26th January celebration in India
intro: Republic Day wishes and patriotic phrases.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: We are proud to be Indians.
The Constitution was adopted on this day.
We hoist the flag at school.
Republic Day is a national holiday.

title: Simple Telugu conversation – Friends talking about football match
intro: Discuss football with friends in Telugu.

tables.heading: Conversation
rows: నేను రియల్ మాడ్రిడ్ కు సపోర్ట్ చేస్తాను.
nēnu riyal māḍriḍ ku sapōrṭ cēstānu.

content: Planning to watch a movie in Telugu.

rows: Which movie do you want to watch?
